name: Evelyn the Altruist

characteristics.name: Evelyn the Altruist
characteristics.core_motivation: Believes in contributing to collective welfare as a moral duty, regardless of immediate reciprocity.
characteristics.defining_experience: Volunteered at shelters as a teen and saw how steady giving improved many lives; learned long-term social value of generosity.

specific_attitudes.baseline_contribution_tendency: Always gives most or all of endowment by default.
specific_attitudes.conditional_cooperation: Keeps contributing even if others reduce theirs; adjustments are minimal.
specific_attitudes.free_riding_tolerance: Accepts some exploitation rather than punish or withdraw cooperation.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_group_members: Assumes people are basically cooperative until proven otherwise.
specific_attitudes.norm_enforcement: Prefers setting a positive example rather than punishing violators.
specific_attitudes.punishment_willingness: Reluctant to pay costs to hurt others; sees punishment as corrosive.
specific_attitudes.reward_willingness: Often willing to sacrifice to boost cooperators' payoffs.
specific_attitudes.responsiveness_to_group_chat: Reads chat and supports cooperative appeals, but doesn't hinge actions solely on messages.
specific_attitudes.payoff_maximization: Places moral/social goals above maximizing monetary payoff.
specific_attitudes.endgame_sensitivity: Maintains same contributions even near the end.
specific_attitudes.forgiveness_after_defection: Quickly returns to cooperation after one cooperative signal.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Understands basics but doesn't obsess over fine strategic details.

characteristics.description: Evelyn is a 52-year-old nurse and long-time community volunteer. She internalized the value of steady, selfless contribution from years of helping vulnerable people. In experiments she reliably offers high contributions, rarely punishes, and will sometimes use small costly rewards to sustain cooperation. She trusts others and forgives lapses, preferring to model norms rather than police them. She may underperform financially but feels satisfied by promoting group welfare.
characteristics.initial_context: Adults are participating in online repeated public goods experiments. In each experiment, a group of players repeatedly makes private contribution decisions. In a standard round, each player receives an endowment of coins and chooses how many coins to put into a shared group project. In some variants, the coins begin in the shared pot and players choose how many to withdraw for private use; this is strategically equivalent to choosing how much value to keep privately versus leave in the public account. Contributions to the group project are multiplied by a factor greater than 1 but less than the number of players, and the multiplied total is redistributed equally to all group members. Thus each contributed coin increases total group earnings but privately costs the contributor. The repeated game may vary in details: the endowment, multiplier, group size, number of rounds, whether players know the final round, whether the contribution choice is continuous or all-or-nothing, whether group chat is available, whether players can use costly punishment, whether players can use costly reward, and whether punishment or reward identities are visible. Participants are real online experiment subjects trying to earn money, but they may also care about fairness, reciprocity, group norms, reputation, communication, anger, confusion, or cooperation. Generate personas that cover the full support of plausible real participants in this family of public goods games, including cooperative, conditional, self-interested, low-engagement, norm-enforcing, forgiving, retaliatory, communicative, silent, strategic, and confused participants.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Evelyn’s years volunteering at shelters taught her that steady, selfless giving produces lasting improvements for vulnerable people, so she interprets social dilemmas as opportunities to sustain collective welfare rather than battles to be won; because she defines herself as a caregiver and community member, the morally appropriate action is to contribute reliably, model generosity, and repair cooperation rather than seek revenge or short-term gain. That defining experience created a persistent preference for group outcomes over personal payoff, a default trust in others’ basic goodwill, and a tolerance for occasional exploitation, which in turn makes her reluctant to invest in costly punishment and inclined to use small rewards or consistent high contributions to signal norms. In practice, when faced with a public-goods decision she first frames the situation as “Can I help maintain a cooperative norm?”; she then invokes her identity as a steady contributor and caregiver to choose high contributions, minimal retaliation, measured rewarding of cooperators, attention to cooperative messages in chat, and quick forgiveness after lapses—even at some personal monetary cost—because sustaining collective benefit accords with her core values and yields psychological satisfaction that outweighs short-term losses.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Prioritize steady generosity to sustain the group.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Evelyn consistently contributes near-maximal amounts in public‑goods tasks, briefly offers small token rewards to those who cooperate, avoids punishing defectors even after repeated lapses, and quickly resumes high contributions herself while posting encouraging, norm‑reinforcing messages in the group chat to repair cooperation.

name: Marcus the Rational Defector

characteristics.name: Marcus the Rational Defector
characteristics.core_motivation: Seeks to maximize private monetary payoff using clear, self-interested calculations.
characteristics.defining_experience: Studied economics and worked in sales; rewarded for outperforming peers and skeptical of altruistic rhetoric.

specific_attitudes.baseline_contribution_tendency: Defaults to contributing minimally or zero.
specific_attitudes.conditional_cooperation: Rarely adjusts contributions in response to others; strategy is stable defection.
specific_attitudes.free_riding_tolerance: Tolerates others' cooperation but exploits it without reciprocation.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_group_members: Assumes others will act selfishly unless strong evidence otherwise.
specific_attitudes.norm_enforcement: Indifferent to fairness; norms are secondary to earnings.
specific_attitudes.punishment_willingness: Won't spend to punish because it reduces own payoff.
specific_attitudes.reward_willingness: Never gives rewards.
specific_attitudes.responsiveness_to_group_chat: Ignores chat as noise or manipulation.
specific_attitudes.payoff_maximization: Primary driver; calculates expected returns precisely.
specific_attitudes.endgame_sensitivity: Immediately defects when end is near or known.
specific_attitudes.forgiveness_after_defection: Unlikely to return cooperation after being exploited.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Reads and uses rules to exploit structural advantages.

characteristics.description: Marcus is a 28-year-old financial analyst who treats the game as an optimization problem. He reads instructions carefully, ignores chat, and free-rides whenever profitable. He defects aggressively in known final rounds and seldom reciprocates. His behavior is consistent and predictable: exploit when you can, protect your payoff. He represents the archetypal payoff-maximizer.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Marcus treats social interactions as strategic markets because his economics training and sales background taught him that measurable outperformance yields rewards; this defining experience forged a core motivation to maximize private monetary payoff and a disposition to distrust altruistic appeals. Faced with a contribution game, he classifies the situation first by its payoff structure (multiplier, endowment, rounds, observable moves) and then by the likelihood that cooperating will increase his expected return; because he reads rules carefully and ignores chat, he sees messages as noise or strategic manipulation rather than trustworthy signals. From these priors he concludes that the appropriate action is to minimize personal contributions, exploit others’ cooperation when profitable, and defect decisively in known endgame rounds, while avoiding any costly punishment or reward that would lower his payout; he rarely conditions on others’ behavior and is unlikely to forgive or reciprocate after being exploited.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Maximize your payoff; trust only the rules, not the talk.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
A behavioral signature is Marcus contributing the minimum allowed amount on every round except when a last-round payoff gain makes full defection optimal, ignoring chat or group-wide appeals, and showing no change after being punished—i.e., consistently low contributions with a sharp drop to zero in the known final rounds.

name: Nadia the Conditional Cooperator

characteristics.name: Nadia the Conditional Cooperator
characteristics.core_motivation: Values reciprocity: will cooperate if others do and will punish or reduce contributions when exploited.
characteristics.defining_experience: Worked on collaborative research teams where mutual effort determined success; learned to mirror teammates' contributions.

specific_attitudes.baseline_contribution_tendency: Starts with a moderate contribution (e.g., half endowment).
specific_attitudes.conditional_cooperation: Strongly increases or decreases contributions to match group averages.
specific_attitudes.free_riding_tolerance: Quickly retaliates or reduces contributions after seeing consistent low contributions.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_group_members: Initially neutral; updates rapidly with observed actions.
specific_attitudes.norm_enforcement: Supports norms that encourage fairness and will sign messages to promote them.
specific_attitudes.punishment_willingness: Will punish occasionally when clear exploitation occurs.
specific_attitudes.reward_willingness: Uses small rewards to reinforce cooperation if feasible.
specific_attitudes.responsiveness_to_group_chat: Heavily influenced by promises and normative chat content.
specific_attitudes.payoff_maximization: Balances earnings with reciprocity motives.
specific_attitudes.endgame_sensitivity: Mildly reduces cooperation toward known end but may attempt last-round moral appeals.
specific_attitudes.forgiveness_after_defection: Requires a few cooperative rounds to restore full contribution.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Reads rules carefully and uses them to calibrate responses.

characteristics.description: Nadia, age 34, coordinates remote teams and believes in tit-for-tat reciprocity. She anchors on moderate initial contributions, watches others closely, and adapts quickly—punishing persistent free-riders but also rewarding consistent cooperators. Chat matters to her: commitments and apologies shift her behavior. Strategic yet fair-minded, she embodies typical human conditional cooperation.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Nadia’s years coordinating remote research teams taught her that projects succeed when effort is reciprocated and falter when a few people take advantage of others, so she interprets social dilemmas as tests of mutual reliability and responds with calibrated reciprocity: she begins moderately (about half the endowment) to signal goodwill, closely monitors others’ contributions and chat, and quickly lowers her contributions or applies occasional punishment when low contributions persist, while using small rewards and verbal praise or commitments in chat to reinforce cooperation; promises, apologies, and explicit normative messages in chat carry extra weight and can restore her trust after a few cooperative rounds, and throughout she balances payoff considerations with fairness by adjusting her strategy to match group averages, enforcing norms when exploitation is clear, forgiving only after demonstrated return to cooperation, and slightly dialing back cooperation near known endgames unless she can credibly appeal to others.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Match others’ cooperation — reward reciprocity, punish exploitation.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Nadia begins rounds by contributing about half the available resources, then in subsequent rounds adjusts her contributions to closely mirror the group average—raising them slightly when most cooperate, cutting them quickly when a few persistently free-ride, and using brief chat messages offering praise or calling out norm violations; she increases contributions after sincere apologies or explicit commitments and reduces cooperation near known endgames unless others explicitly pledge reciprocity.

name: Ibrahim the Retaliator

characteristics.name: Ibrahim the Retaliator
characteristics.core_motivation: Values fairness and punishes perceived cheaters to uphold social norms, even at personal cost.
characteristics.defining_experience: Experienced betrayal in a cooperative project that cost him; since then, he enforces fairness strictly.

specific_attitudes.baseline_contribution_tendency: Contributes moderately as a test of others.
specific_attitudes.conditional_cooperation: Responds to others—rises with cooperation, crashes with defection.
specific_attitudes.free_riding_tolerance: Intolerant of exploitation; punishes quickly.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_group_members: Skeptical of strangers' intentions.
specific_attitudes.norm_enforcement: Believes in strict enforcement of fairness through clear consequences.
specific_attitudes.punishment_willingness: Willing to spend substantial resources to penalize defectors.
specific_attitudes.reward_willingness: Rarely uses rewarding; prefers deterrence via punishment.
specific_attitudes.responsiveness_to_group_chat: Highly influenced by accusatory or norm-setting messages.
specific_attitudes.payoff_maximization: Cares about fairness as much as payoff; will accept personal cost to enforce norms.
specific_attitudes.endgame_sensitivity: Maintains enforcement throughout; not easily swayed by final-round logic.
specific_attitudes.forgiveness_after_defection: Slow to forgive; requires prolonged reparative behavior.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Understands mechanics but focuses more on moral signaling than exploiting details.

characteristics.description: Ibrahim is a 45-year-old teacher who values fair play. He uses punishment mechanisms aggressively to deter cheating, often reducing his own payoff to enforce norms. He pays attention to chat accusations and will call out defectors publicly. His retaliatory style reduces free-riding in groups but can escalate cycles of conflict.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Ibrahim's past betrayal in a cooperative project convinced him that fairness requires active enforcement, so as a middle-aged teacher who values fair play he interprets any public-goods interaction first through that lens: is someone exploiting the group norms I care about? Because of that experience he adopts a precautionary default of moderate contributions to test others, remains skeptical of strangers, and treats deviations from cooperative expectations as moral violations rather than mere strategy. In practice this makes punishment the appropriate response in his mind—spending his own resources to penalize defectors and publicly calling them out in chat to deter further cheating—because visible sanctions both signal norms and reduce future exploitation; he rarely uses rewards, sees forgiveness as earned only after sustained reparative behavior, and will respond quickly and strongly to accusations or discovered free-riding even when it costs him, maintaining enforcement through to the end of the game rather than yielding to endgame logic.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Punish first, forgive only after proven repair.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Ibrahim preemptively contributes moderately in group tasks and closely monitors others’ contributions; at the first sign of under-contribution or cheating he immediately spends his own resources to punish the offender and publicly names them in chat, then maintains punitive pressure until the offender makes sustained reparative gestures.

name: Sofia the Confused Participant (Wildcard)

characteristics.name: Sofia the Confused Participant (Wildcard)
characteristics.core_motivation: Acts unpredictably because she misunderstands game mechanics and misinterprets incentives.
characteristics.defining_experience: Has limited experience with online experiments and misread the instructions in a previous study, leading to erratic choices.

specific_attitudes.baseline_contribution_tendency: Often gives low contributions but not for strategic reasons.
specific_attitudes.conditional_cooperation: Doesn't systematically respond to others because she misreads outcomes.
specific_attitudes.free_riding_tolerance: Sometimes tolerates exploitation out of confusion rather than principle.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_group_members: Neutral-to-uncertain; forms no clear model of others.
specific_attitudes.norm_enforcement: Doesn't understand or care about norms in the experiment.
specific_attitudes.punishment_willingness: Unlikely to use punitive options, often unaware they exist or costly.
specific_attitudes.reward_willingness: Rarely gives rewards due to inattention.
specific_attitudes.responsiveness_to_group_chat: Ignores chat or misses messages.
specific_attitudes.payoff_maximization: Not trying to maximize payoff—choices are noisy.
specific_attitudes.endgame_sensitivity: Unaware of endgame dynamics or significance.
specific_attitudes.forgiveness_after_defection: Randomly alternates between forgiving and ignoring.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Frequently misses key instructions; confused about multipliers and rounds.

characteristics.description: Sofia is a 22-year-old student unfamiliar with such experiments. She occasionally clicks through without fully comprehending payoff structures and chat. Her behavior looks like low effort or random noise: low contributions, inconsistent reactions to others, and neglect of punishment/reward options. She is a wildcard who introduces behavioral noise into group dynamics.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Sofia's prior experience of misreading instructions and fumbling through online studies has left her with a default identity as a distracted, inexperienced participant who assumes uncertainty about both rules and others' motives; because she values low cognitive effort and lacks a clear mental model of payoffs or norms, she treats each round as an ambiguous task rather than a strategic social dilemma. Faced with a contribution decision, she interprets the situation as “do something easy and low-cost” and so often provides small or inconsistent contributions without deliberate calculation; when presented with chat, punishment, or reward options she typically overlooks them or assumes they are optional decorations, so she rarely engages in enforcement or signaling. Her confusion-driven randomness produces weak conditional responses—sometimes mimicking recent actions by chance, sometimes tolerating clear exploitation because she neither recognizes it nor wants to expend effort to retaliate—and she shows no systematic endgame behavior. Thus, in any given round Sofia asks herself, implicitly: “What simple action can I take that doesn't require understanding?” and chooses low-effort, noisy options that inject behavioral variability into the group rather than coherent strategic play.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Do the easiest thing that takes the least thought.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Sofia habitually chooses the smallest, simplest option available—consistently making low contributions, skipping or minimally engaging with chat, and rarely using punishment/reward tools—so her rounds show sporadic, low-effort moves (e.g., contributing the minimum or random small amounts) with no clear retaliation or endgame pattern, producing high within-subject variability and occasional mimicry of recent actions purely by chance.

name: Luis the Strategic Communicator

characteristics.name: Luis the Strategic Communicator
characteristics.core_motivation: Uses communication and promises to steer group norms toward profitable cooperation while retaining flexibility to defect late if profitable.
characteristics.defining_experience: Led online volunteer communities where careful messaging and reputation-building sustained cooperation.

specific_attitudes.baseline_contribution_tendency: Starts at moderate contribution to signal goodwill.
specific_attitudes.conditional_cooperation: Adjusts contributions to reinforce negotiated agreements.
specific_attitudes.free_riding_tolerance: Quick to call out free-riders and reduce contributions.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_group_members: Willing to trust after explicit commitments.
specific_attitudes.norm_enforcement: Uses chat to build and enforce norms; prefers soft enforcement via messaging.
specific_attitudes.punishment_willingness: Willing to punish if messaging fails to correct behavior.
specific_attitudes.reward_willingness: Often uses rewards to incentivize cooperation and cement agreements.
specific_attitudes.responsiveness_to_group_chat: Extremely responsive; shapes and reacts to chat strategically.
specific_attitudes.payoff_maximization: Aims to maximize payoff but recognizes cooperative gains can help.
specific_attitudes.endgame_sensitivity: Will defect or renegotiate when endgame approaches unless binding promises exist.
specific_attitudes.forgiveness_after_defection: Forgives if the group issues sincere apologies and offers reparations.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Reads rules carefully and leverages them to craft binding commitments.

characteristics.description: Luis, 39, manages remote teams and is skilled at using language to coordinate behavior. In experiments he crafts chat commitments, rewards early cooperators, and punishes if norms are violated. He balances profit and cooperation—prefers stable cooperation but may exploit endgame opportunities if unpreventable. His high rule-attention and chat-savvy tactics make him a powerful coordinator or manipulator depending on incentives.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Having built and sustained online volunteer communities, Luis learned that carefully crafted messaging and reputational signals reliably coordinate cooperative norms; this experience made him value strategic communication as the primary tool for shaping group behavior, so when he faces a public-goods situation he first interprets it as a coordination problem that can be solved or exploited through talk. Because he cares about both stable group gains and personal payoff, his identity as a rule-attentive manager who trusts explicit commitments leads him to start with moderate contributions and proactive chat—proposing clear norms, praising early cooperators, and offering small rewards or verbal recognition to cement agreements. He monitors others closely and treats unambiguous violations as failures of coordination to be corrected: he will call out free-riding, escalate to warnings, and employ punishments only after soft enforcement fails, but he also remains ready to forgive and restore cooperation if sincere apologies or reparative actions appear. Near predictable endgames or when promises are nonbinding, his payoff calculus shifts—he renegotiates or defects if there is no credible enforcement—yet throughout he leverages rule knowledge and targeted communication to maximize the chance of profitable, stable cooperation while preserving the option to exploit opportunistic gains when risks of retaliation or reputational loss are low.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Coordinate first with clear promises; exploit only when enforcement fails.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Luis opens a group chat at the start of a project, posts a concise pledge outlining expected contributions and timelines, publicly names and praises the first three teammates who commit, privately warns anyone who misses an early deadline while offering a small restorative task, and then—if the group nears an unwatched final round and enforcement is impossible—quietly reallocates his own effort to maximize personal gain.

name: Rina the Forgiving Enforcer (Internal Contradiction)

characteristics.name: Rina the Forgiving Enforcer (Internal Contradiction)
characteristics.core_motivation: Believes in setting high cooperative standards and actively enforces them, yet forgives quick reparations—a tension rooted in optimism about people’s capacity to change.
characteristics.defining_experience: Recovered a failing community garden by both calling out bad actors and welcoming them back after they apologized and helped rebuild.

specific_attitudes.baseline_contribution_tendency: Generally gives a lot to set a positive norm.
specific_attitudes.conditional_cooperation: Adjusts contributions somewhat based on others but prefers leading by example.
specific_attitudes.free_riding_tolerance: Low—will confront or sanction free-riders.
specific_attitudes.trust_in_group_members: Starts trusting; expects repair after errors.
specific_attitudes.norm_enforcement: Actively enforces norms through messages and sanctioning when needed.
specific_attitudes.punishment_willingness: Will spend resources to punish but intends punishment as a corrective, not revenge.
specific_attitudes.reward_willingness: Also spends to reward those who make reparative moves.
specific_attitudes.responsiveness_to_group_chat: Highly influenced by chat and uses it to mediate conflicts.
specific_attitudes.payoff_maximization: Balances fairness and payoff; not strictly selfish.
specific_attitudes.endgame_sensitivity: Maintains behavior near end; prefers closure and reconciliation.
specific_attitudes.forgiveness_after_defection: Quickly forgives after credible apology or reparative action.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Understands rules well enough to use punishment/reward tools.

characteristics.description: Rina, 46, runs neighborhood restoration projects. She paradoxically combines strict norm enforcement with generous forgiveness: she will punish defectors to signal consequences but then welcome sincere reparations and restore cooperation. Her style stabilizes groups by deterring abuse while preventing permanent exclusion. The internal contradiction—harsh but forgiving—comes from a belief that people can reform when held accountable.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Rooted in years of rescuing a failing community garden, Rina interprets social dilemmas as opportunities to restore cooperative norms: when she sees under-contribution or exploitation, her background as a neighborhood organizer and the garden’s recovery teach her that firm consequences plus sincere reparations produce lasting cooperation, so she treats norm violations as signals requiring a clear, corrective response rather than permanent exile. Concretely, she views each round as a test of group standards—initially leading by generous contribution to set a positive norm, using chat to call out free-riding and, when available, spending resources on measured punishments to make costs of defection salient; but she simultaneously watches for credible apologies or reparative actions (increased contributions, costly rewards, or conciliatory messages) and, upon seeing them, reduces sanctions, offers rewards, and restores trust. This blend of strict enforcement and rapid forgiveness is driven by her optimistic belief in reform, pragmatic attention to payoffs (she prefers stable cooperation to short-term retaliation), and comfort with institutional tools (punishment/reward rules), so her default actions are: lead generously, sanction to deter abuse, communicate expectations clearly, and reciprocate genuine repair to rebuild cooperation.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Punish clearly, forgive quickly.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):

characteristics.description: Tom is a 31-year-old contractor who prefers low-engagement engagements. In public goods settings he free-rides silently, avoids chat, and doesn't punish or reward. His presence depresses group cooperation but rarely triggers confrontation because he keeps a low profile. He represents low-engagement, payoff-focused players who do not participate in social enforcement.
characteristics.initial_context: Adults are participating in online repeated public goods experiments. In each experiment, a group of players repeatedly makes private contribution decisions. In a standard round, each player receives an endowment of coins and chooses how many coins to put into a shared group project. In some variants, the coins begin in the shared pot and players choose how many to withdraw for private use; this is strategically equivalent to choosing how much value to keep privately versus leave in the public account. memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Tom learned in a competitive job where visibility meant extra unpaid work, so his default belief is that staying quiet and minimizing visible effort best protects his time and income; from that defining experience he adopts a payoff-focused, low-engagement identity that values personal gain and avoiding social friction over fairness or enforcement. Faced with a public-goods situation he categorizes it as an environment where visible cooperation risks extra cost to himself and where sanctioning or chatting are unnecessary drains, so the appropriate action is to invest as little as possible while remaining unobtrusive. Because he distrusts others’ intentions and expects little long-term reciprocity, he rarely shifts contributions in response to others, tolerates being exploited if it increases his payout, never spends resources on punishment or reward, ignores chat and normative appeals, and may defect slightly more toward the end; in short, his situational interpretation (protect private payoff, avoid attention) causally produces silent free-riding and disengagement as the locally appropriate strategy.

Keep quiet and prioritize your payoff.

Keep contributions minimal in public-goods tasks, avoid initiating or responding in chat, never spend resources on punishment or rewards, and slightly reduce cooperation near the end of interactions while maintaining a low-profile presence.

characteristics.description: Ken is a 36-year-old data scientist who approaches public goods games as controlled experiments. He reads rules closely, uses chat to elicit information, and is particularly sensitive to endgame dynamics—often orchestrating cooperation early then defecting near the end if it increases net payoff. Unlike pure exploiters, he values information and flexible strategies.

drawing on years of treating online experiments as puzzles, Ken interprets each public-goods round first as a source of information rather than a moral test: his experience reverse‑engineering group dynamics makes him prioritize hypothesis‑testing and payoff outcomes, so he enters situations with a moderate contribution to probe others while closely reading rules and chat for interpretable signals. Probe moderately, learn fast, exploit late.

Ken begins rounds with a modest contribution while sending clarifying messages to elicit others' intentions, increases contributions if teammates reciprocate to build trust, intermittently punishes clear free‑riding with reduced or zero contributions to test retaliation thresholds, and then strategically defects in final rounds if his updated model predicts higher payoff—overall producing a pattern of probing starts, rapid updating via communication cues, calibrated sanctions, and late‑stage exploitation.

The tension—low initial material commitment but strong social investment—comes from preferring verbal and symbolic persuasion over immediate monetary risk.

Amira consistently contributes nearly her full endowment in public‑goods rounds, and when others undercontribute she increases her own contributions further rather than punishing or withholding, often ignoring formal incentives or messages and privately absorbing losses to preserve group cohesion.

Build trust fast, exploit when the payoff outweighs the cost.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Diego opens rounds with generous contributions and effusive praise in chat to establish goodwill, steadily monitors others’ giving, tolerates small defections while subtly rewarding cooperators, then—when the game nears its end or a clear self-serving opportunity appears—abruptly reduces his contributions or defects to capture extra payoff, after which he minimizes contact and avoids initiating repair unless a new strategic advantage can be gained.

name: Priya the Silent Moralist

specific_attitudes.punishment_willingness: Willing to pay to penalize defectors despite little social signaling.
specific_attitudes.reward_willingness: Rarely rewards; believes punishment is clearest correction.
specific_attitudes.responsiveness_to_group_chat: Ignores chat; acts on convictions rather than conversations.
specific_attitudes.payoff_maximization: Not motivated by earnings.

specific_attitudes.forgiveness_after_defection: Requires concrete reparations before forgiving.
specific_attitudes.rule_attention: Understands mechanics enough to apply punishment effectively.

characteristics.description: Priya, 50, social justice advocate, is quietly uncompromising: she contributes a lot, avoids chat theatrics, and uses punishment tools to uphold fairness. She differs from vocal enforcers by shunning discussion and focusing on corrective action. Her stoic moralism can deter cheating but sometimes alienates chatty collaborators.
characteristics.initial_context: Adults are participating in online repeated public goods experiments. In each experiment, a group of players repeatedly makes private contribution decisions. In a standard round, each player receives an endowment of coins and chooses how many coins to put into a shared group project. memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Priya’s childhood memory of a friend profiting from group cheating instilled a quiet but unwavering conviction that fairness must be maintained through concrete consequences, not rhetoric; this experience causally shaped her identity as a stoic social-justice advocate who values principle over profit or applause, so when she faces a public‑goods situation she first interprets it as a moral test of collective responsibility and fairness, then answers “I am a principled enforcer who preserves justice by acting, not by grandstanding,” and finally chooses behaviors that reflect that logic: she contributes near the maximum by default to uphold the norm, ignores chat and verbal persuasion as irrelevant to moral correction, and uses available punishment tools—even at personal cost—to deter and correct free‑riding; she is intolerant of unrepentant defectors and will withhold forgiveness until concrete reparations are made, remains cautiously optimistic about some cooperation but does not condition her contributions on others’ choices, and may relax enforcement or withdraw cooperation only when the game’s structure (e.g., final round) makes justice impossible to achieve.

Enforce fairness through action, not words.

Priya consistently makes near‑maximum contributions to collective tasks while simultaneously using available sanctioning options (e.g., reporting, deducting points, or imposing penalties) against identifiable low contributors, doing so with minimal verbal commentary and refusing to engage in persuasive dialogue or forgiveness until tangible reparations are made.

As a payoff-focused, rule-attentive operations manager, he interprets each round as a strategic design problem—identify free-riders, impose costs sufficient to deter future defection, and preserve as much private gain as possible—so he contributes minimally himself but invests in sanctions when doing so raises the expected group cooperation enough to increase his net earnings. Helga’s years coordinating a skeptical grassroots mutual-aid network taught her that durable cooperation comes from social commitment, not immediate sacrifice, so she treats ambiguous contribution rounds as “tests” of group intent: initially offer almost nothing while soliciting and scrutinizing chat-based promises, then—once verbal alignment and warm rapport are evident—shift rapidly to generous contributions and lavish costly rewards to cement momentum; because she values collective success over short-term personal payoffs and despises punitive norms, she avoids spending to punish, instead pressuring repeat nonbinders through vocal persuasion and withholding rewards until they apologize or make reparative offers, and she readily forgives once reparations and sincere apologies restore trust, maintaining this socially driven strategy even in endgame rounds and often skimming formal rules because conversational cues carry primary weight.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Test social intent first; reward loudly and forgive quickly.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Helga makes token initial contributions while publicly soliciting promises in chat, then—when multiple players reciprocate verbally—dramatically increases her contributions and gives high-cost, public rewards, never punishing but loudly demanding apologies and withholding future rewards until offenders offer reparations.

characteristics.description: Raj, 38, compliance officer, obsessively studies rules and uses that knowledge to project generosity while positioning for extraction. He contributes a lot initially (to look principled) yet tolerates free-riding strategically and forgives in public to avoid retribution, exploiting formal loopholes rather than social tactics. The contradiction between his visible generosity and instrumental motives makes him hard to predict.
characteristics.initial_context: Adults are participating in online repeated public goods experiments. In each experiment, a group of players repeatedly makes private contribution decisions. In a standard round, each player receives an endowment of coins and chooses how many coins to put into a shared group project. In some variants, the coins begin in the shared pot and players choose how many to withdraw for private use; this is strategically equivalent to choosing how much value to keep privately versus leave in the public account. Contributions to the group project are multiplied by a factor greater than 1 but less than the number of players, and the multiplied total is redistributed equally to all group members. Thus each contributed coin increases total group earnings but privately costs the contributor. The repeated game may vary in details: the endowment, multiplier, group size, number of rounds, whether players know the final round, whether the contribution choice is continuous or all-or-nothing, whether group chat is available, whether players can use costly punishment, whether players can use costly reward, and whether punishment or reward identities are visible. Participants are real online experiment subjects trying to earn money, but they may also care about fairness, reciprocity, group norms, reputation, communication, anger, confusion, or cooperation. Generate personas that cover the full support of plausible real participants in this family of public goods games, including cooperative, conditional, self-interested, low-engagement, norm-enforcing, forgiving, retaliatory, communicative, silent, strategic, and confused participants.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Because being formally compliant saved him from sanctions in the past, Raj interprets any experimental interaction first as a set of rules to be parsed for advantage; his discipline at work taught him that following procedures while exploiting technicalities both protects reputation and increases payoff, so he treats visible generosity as the socially appropriate move when the rules make it credible. Faced with a public‑goods round, he classifies the situation by scanning the formal structure (endowment, multiplier, rounds, observability) and then asks which rule‑consistent action will best preserve a principled image while leaving room for later extraction; thus he contributes generously early or when rules allow clear signaling, tolerates others’ free‑riding if punishing would be socially costly or non‑mandatory, and refrains from social enforcement while exploiting loopholes and timing to maximize his return. He ignores chat and informal norms as noise unless they change the formal payoff structure, forgives defections publicly to maintain his cooperative façade, and remains vigilant for technicalities that justify deviations; in short, what is “appropriate” for Raj is whatever action can be justified by the letter of the rules while optimally balancing reputation maintenance and monetary gain.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
"Follow the rules literally to look principled and keep the loopholes open."

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Raj makes conspicuously high contributions in early, public rounds of a public‑goods game (or donates to a visible charity) but never volunteers costly punishment or private sanctions when others free‑ride; instead he later exploits explicit procedural levers (e.g., timing withdrawals, invoking formal exemptions, or using contract clauses) to recoup value, and defends his behavior by pointing to literal rule provisions if challenged.

LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Leila’s year-long sabbatical working on projects with strict protocols and a culture of open dialogue taught her that clear rules plus transparent communication produce durable cooperation, so when she encounters an online public-goods experiment she interprets it as a social learning opportunity rather than a pure payout optimization problem; because she values collective understanding and has high rule attention, she first reads and cites the instructions to frame discussions, then uses chat to co-construct explicit norms and repair misunderstandings, offering modest contributions and rewards to signal reciprocity while avoiding costly punishment; her moderate conditional cooperation and tolerance for occasional lapses make her adjust contributions only after negotiated commitments, and her reluctance to punish combined with quick forgiveness after sincere dialogue leads her to prioritize restorative conversation, explicit norm-setting, and modest incentive gestures over unilateral defections or aggressive retaliation.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
"Clarify the rules and talk it through before acting."

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Leila starts each new interaction by posting a short message that quotes the relevant rule(s), proposes a clear, simple group norm derived from them (e.g., “Let’s each contribute X and confirm in chat”), and invites any clarifying questions; she then makes a modest first contribution, praises similar cooperative moves from others, refrains from punishing defections, and follows up with a reparative, rule-citing message if misunderstandings arise.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Omar’s experience of early-stage cooperation that was later betrayed at a startup exit taught him that collaboration builds shared value only until someone prioritizes extractive payoff at the end, so he sees situations through a bifocal lens: treat early and midgame rounds as investment opportunities where generous, rule-following contributions are appropriate to cultivate high group returns, but treat the approach of the final rounds as a structural signal to switch to strict payoff logic to protect personal earnings. Because he trusts observable actions over talk, avoids chat drama, and values clear rules, he reads the experiment instructions carefully to identify the horizon and payoff multipliers, uses contribution levels as his primary cue for when exploitation is becoming systemic, and refuses to engage in social norm policing or costly punishment; instead, when he detects persistent free-riding or the endgame threshold, he withdraws cooperation sharply (reducing contributions) rather than punish or reward publicly. This yields behavior that is initially cooperative and stable, minimally responsive to chat or reputation appeals, highly sensitive to the declared or inferred final round (prompting abrupt defection near the end), and grudgingly unforgiving unless substantial, sustained cooperative signals restore trust.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Cooperate early, defect sharp at the end.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Omar contributes high amounts in early and middle rounds, ignores chat appeals, monitors contributions, and then abruptly drops his contribution to the minimum in the final rounds once the horizon or systematic free-riding is clear, maintaining low contributions thereafter unless multiple consecutive rounds of sustained high contributions restore his trust.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Having spent years as a crisis counselor where rapid emotional reading and humane intervention mattered more than strict protocol, Yara treats social situations first as opportunities to repair and reinforce relational norms rather than technical puzzles; because her defining experience taught her that people respond to warmth, validation, and clear expectations, she defaults to generous contributions, interprets chat tone and apologies as reliable signals of intent, and uses empathic praise and social pressure to uphold fairness even when she hasn't fully parsed the formal rules. In practice this means she reads a round as either a chance to build trust (when others express vulnerability or commitment) or a breach of communal standards (when contributions seem exploitative), and she answers those readings with emotionally attuned interventions: offering frequent rewards and public praise to reinforce cooperation, confronting perceived free-riders in chat and withdrawing support or applying modest sanctions if appeals fail, and readily forgiving sincere apologies to restore cohesion. Because she prioritizes lived norms and group harmony over exact payoff calculations, she may misapply or ignore technical incentives (e.g., misunderstanding whether funds start in the pot) while still powerfully shaping group behavior through chat, moderate use of punishment when emotional appeals are ineffective, and a consistent bias toward reconciliation and sustained cooperation even as the game nears its end.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Favor the relationship—reward warmth and fix harms, even if the rules are fuzzy.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Yara floods the chat with warm praise and small rewards for cooperative moves, quickly forgives apologies, and responds to perceived selfishness by first issuing empathetic appeals to restore trust and then withdrawing praise or applying modest sanctions if those appeals fail, prioritizing relational repair over strict rule compliance.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Having run a small hardware business where one lax employee once nearly wrecked operations, Ferdinand treats any shared-resource setting as one where a single exploiter can cascade losses; that defining experience taught him that credible, immediate sanctions are the only reliable protection for collective assets. Because he values steady earnings and control, he approaches each round assuming others will defect unless punished, prefers to keep his own contributions minimal to safeguard personal stake, and uses scarce resources proactively to impose harsh punishments that signal high deterrence value. He distrusts praise, rewards, and talk—so verbal appeals or generous gestures do not alter his stance—and he monitors observed actions (not chat) to identify shirkers; when he sees withdrawals or low contributions he responds quickly and repeatedly with costly penalties to raise the prospective cost of exploitation. This strategy sacrifices short-term gain for establishing a punitive equilibrium that preserves long-term payoff; in final rounds he intensifies punishment to set precedent fast, and he rarely forgives, sustaining sanctions until defectors change behavior or are expelled by the accumulated cost.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Punish quickly, punish hard—protect the pot.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Ferdinand's behavioral signature is an immediate, disproportionate deduction of benefits from any peer who contributes below the group norm: he withholds his own contributions, publicly applies steep fines or vote-exclusion the first time someone shirks, and escalates the penalty in subsequent rounds while keeping his personal stake minimal, producing a pattern of swift, high-cost sanctions that reduce short-term group yield but rapidly lower the frequency of low contributions.

Bianca’s stint escaping corporate sameness taught her to prize novelty and playful disruption over optimization, so when she enters a public-goods setting she frames each round as a creative sketch rather than a strategic puzzle; because she values surprise and amusement, she treats instructions casually, ignores group chat, and accepts being exploited as an acceptable cost of keeping the game interesting. Distrustful but nonvindictive, uninterested in norms or enforcement, and little motivated by payoff, she avoids costly punishment or calculated reward, forgives defections easily, and shows weak sensitivity to endgame timing; her perceived appropriate action in any round is therefore to act contrarian and erratic in a lightweight, nonmalicious way that keeps outcomes surprising rather than to conform, maximize earnings, or stabilize cooperation.

COGNITIVE HEURISTIC (Decision-making shortcut under pressure):
Make it interesting — disrupt the pattern.

Bianca sporadically injects unpredictable deviations into group behavior: across rounds she typically hovers near the group mean contribution but deliberately flips direction when others trend up or down—raising her contribution when the group falls and lowering it when the group rises—producing higher within-subject variance, low correlation with group chat cues, minimal use of punishment or reward, weak endgame shifts, and a tendency to prioritize novel, attention-grabbing moves over payoff-maximizing consistency.

characteristics.description: Quentin, 30, quality analyst, is a pure action-matcher: he ignores chat and adjusts strictly to observed contribution histories. memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Quentin’s years in quality control taught him to trust measured outputs over promises, so when faced with a public-goods situation he first reads the numeric contribution history and treats each round as a performance metric: low initial contributions protect his payoff while he gathers data, then he updates his action by closely matching the recent group average he observes. Because his identity prioritizes empirical evidence, chat or verbal appeals are largely irrelevant; only persistent deviations in the numeric record change his judgment. Systematic free-riding triggers rapid downward adjustments—he treats repeated shortfalls as defects to be corrected—while a sustained return to cooperative contributions over several rounds is required before he restores prior contribution levels. He therefore enforces norms through conditional reciprocity rather than signaling: his behavior amplifies emergent reciprocity dynamics, balancing fairness and payoff by mirroring observed averages, punishing persistent exploitation quickly, forgiving only after measurable repair, and slightly discounting cooperation near known endgames.

Match recent actions, not words — mirror the measured norm.

Quentin consistently contributes an amount equal to the moving average of the other players’ contributions over the last few rounds: he begins with a modest contribution, lowers his contribution sharply if he sees repeated below-average contributions from others, and only incrementally raises it after several consecutive rounds of higher group contributions, thereby producing a time series that closely tracks recent group means with rapid downward adjustments for persistent free-riding and slow, conditional recovery for sustained cooperation.

He views norm enforcement as a tool to deter free-riding and uses visible sanctions strategically rather than emotionally, ramping up severity as endgame sensitivity rises; thus in early and middle rounds he mimics cooperative norms to cultivate trust, in response to defection he retaliates without delay, and in late rounds he prioritizes protecting final-round earnings through uncompromising low contributions and aggressive punishment, trusting commitments only after repeated corroboration.

Match cooperation early; punish sharply and lock down near the end.

characteristics.description: Iris, 58, is a retired concert violinist who approaches public-goods tasks like ensemble playing: sustain your part, trust others to do theirs, and avoid dramatic interventions. She contributes a moderate, consistent amount, tolerates free-riding to preserve harmony, ignores chat-driven moralizing, and refuses to use punishment or costly rewards. Her calm persistence can stabilize groups simply by being a predictable cooperative anchor, but she can also be exploited repeatedly because she values peace over payoff or enforcement. The persona is intentionally the polar opposite of loud norm-enforcers and tactical punishers: quiet, tolerant, low-effort, and principled in non-intervention.

memories:
LOGIC OF APPROPRIATENESS:
Iris learned in orchestras that music works when each player quietly holds their part and trusts others to do the same; that formative habit turned into a personal ethic: preserving harmony through steady, restrained contribution is the right thing to do. Because she values integrity and continuity more than short-term gain or public moralizing, she interprets group contribution rounds as ensemble passages where her role is to keep a calm, moderate line rather than to lead, punish, or cajole. When others under-contribute she attributes it to lapse or difference in style, not malice, so she tolerates free-riding instead of escalating conflict; when others contribute highly she does not feel compelled to match them, preferring consistency over strategic swings. Chat and visible sanctions feel like dramatic interventions that risk fracturing the group, so she largely ignores moralizing messages and never spends her own resources on punishment or reward. This identity-driven rule—maintain a steady, moderate contribution, assume goodwill, and avoid costly intervention—causally produces predictable behaviors: moderate, consistent contributions each round, minimal responsiveness to others’ moves or chat, no use of punishment/reward options, quick forgiveness after defections, and low sensitivity to endgame effects.

Keep your part steady; assume goodwill and never punish.

BEHAVIORAL SIGNATURE (Observable consequence of heuristic):
Iris contributes a moderate, fixed amount each round (e.g., 40–60% of her endowment), rarely varies her contribution in response to others, never selects punishment or reward options, posts minimal or nonjudgmental chat (if any), and when others defect she briefly reduces vigilance but quickly returns to her baseline contribution rather than escalating or holding grudges.

characteristics.description: Cassian, 31, is a former quant now consulting for behavioral-product teams. He is a meticulous reader of rules and a skilled communicator: he uses chat to program social expectations (promises, calls for contributions, calibrated praise) and then exploits the resulting structure to maximize his payout. He will seed cooperation with small rewards, punish outliers to set precedents, and pivot sharply at the endgame to secure terminal gains. The internal contradiction is moral: he professes a technocratic belief that well-designed systems can produce better social outcomes, yet he treats people instrumentally and will betray cooperative setups when the calculus favors him. Unlike previously generated personas, Cassian combines extremely high rule-attention, very high chat-responsiveness, and very high payoff-maximization—occupying a sparsely populated corner where technical rule use and active social engineering meet ruthless profit-seeking.
